title: Shipfix-Global Market Update
summary:
Main
By Ulf Bergman
Dry bulk freight rates advanced last week, with the BDI gaining more than eleven per cent. While all segments were in the black, the Capesizes were mainly responsible for the headline index's solid performance. A stronger dollar contributed to headwinds for many commodities, but crude oil went against the flow amid renewed concerns over global supplies.

children.title: Main
children.summary:
By Ulf Bergman
Dry bulk freight rates advanced last week, with the BDI gaining more than eleven per cent. While all segments were in the black, the Capesizes were mainly responsible for the headline index's solid performance.

children.title: Macro/Geopolitics
children.summary: The US dollar continued to strengthen last week as markets digested news that new unemployment claims in the US fell to their lowest in over six months. The development increased the likelihood of a further US interest rate hike in the coming months.

children.title: Commodity Markets
children.summary: Crude oil advanced last week following news that Saudi Arabia and Russia are extending their unilateral production curbs. The November Brent futures increased by 2.1 per cent over the course of the week, settling at 90.45 dollars per barrel on Friday.

children.title: Freight and Bunker Markets
children.summary: The Baltic Dry Index advanced by 11.4 per cent over the course of the past week. While all dry bulk segments had a week in the black, much of the headline index's performance originated from the Capesizes.

children.title: The View from the Shipfix Desk
section_path: The View from the Shipfix Desk
children.section_path_text: The View from the Shipfix Desk
children.summary: Even if the optimists among us suggest that the recent Chinese data are pointing towards a stabilisation of the world's second-largest economy, the country's post-COVID recovery has failed to match the high expectations on display at the beginning of the year. As a result of the underwhelming rebound and the continued travails for the country's beleaguered property sector, Chinese demand for steel has been under pressure for much of the year.
